content.problemStatement:
You have given a set of digits which you can use (multiple number of times) to make two numbers aaa and bbb  (a<b)(a \lt b)(a<b) such that (b−a)(b-a)(b−a) is divisible by given number XXX.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
First line of each testcase contains of a single integer NNN - number of digits in the set and XXX
Next line contains NNN space separated integers in increasing order D1,D2,D3,......,DND_1, D_2, D_3, ......, D_ND1​,D2​,D3​,......,DN​


Output Format

For each test case, output a single line containing two integers aaa and bbb.
If multiple answer are possible then minimise bbb, if still multiple answer are possible then minimise aaa.
constraints: Sum of X over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and X – the number of allowed digits and the divisor. The second line contains N distinct digits D1 D2 … DN (1 ≤ Di ≤ 9) in increasing order. Digits may be used any number of times to form decimal numbers (no leading zeros because 0 is never an allowed digit).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with two integers a and b separated by a space, where a < b, both numbers consist only of the allowed digits, (b‑a) is divisible by X, b is minimal possible and, among those, a is minimal possible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : 
First 5 numbers that can be formed using 1 and 2 are - 1 2 11 12 21 22

Take b=1b = 1b=1 : Can't take any value of aaa with satisfies the above conditions.
Take b=2b = 2b=2 : Again, can't take any value of aaa.
Take b=11b = 11b=11 : Now, aaa must be taken as 2 as (11−2)=9(11 - 2) = 9(11−2)=9 is divisible by 9.

From above steps we can verify that bbb or aaa can't be minimised further. 
Test Case 2 : a=333a = 333a=333 and b=3333b = 3333b=3333 is the correct answer as (3333-333) = 3000 is divisible by 8.
It can be proved that neither bbb nor aaa can be minimised further.

solution.approach: For a fixed test case let S be the set of allowed digits. While generating numbers that consist only of digits from S, we keep them in increasing numeric order. This can be done with a min‑heap: initially push every single‑digit number d∈S. Repeatedly pop the smallest number cur, compute r = cur mod X. If we have already seen this residue, let a be the first (and therefore smallest) number that produced r; cur is the smallest possible b that shares the same residue, so (cur‑a) is divisible by X and a<b. Because numbers are processed in strictly increasing order, the first time a repeated residue appears gives the optimal pair (minimal b, then minimal a). If the residue is new we store cur as the first occurrence for r and generate all children cur*10 + d (for every d∈S) and push them into the heap. By the pigeonhole principle after at most X+1 generated numbers a residue must repeat, guaranteeing termination. The algorithm runs in O(X log X) time (heap operations) and O(X) memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(X log X) per test case (X ≤ 10^5, sum of X over all cases ≤ 10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(X) for the residue table and the heap.
